title:
The Sacred Naga in Khmer Culture: Serpentine Symbol of Protection, Pow
– HD Asian Art
highlights: the Naga, a mythological serpent that  ...    Found across temple balustrades, lintels, pediments, and ceremonial stairways, the Naga is not merely decorative—it is a guardian of sacred space, a protector of divine order, and a symbol of royal legitimacy and cosmic balance.  ...    Nowhere is the importance of the Naga more visible than in Khmer temple architecture, particularly during the Angkor period (9th–15th centuries), when monumental temples like Angkor Wat, Bayon, and Baphuon were constructed as cosmic microcosms. Naga balustrades, typically with five or seven heads, line temple causeways and stairways. These multi-headed serpents are often shown flaring dramatically at the ends, their bodies stretching like a coiling guardrail along sacred paths. These balustrades symbolize the Naga as guardian of thresholds, marking the transition between the secular world and  ...    The seven-headed Naga is particularly prominent in Angkor Wat and Preah Khan, where it functions as a symbol

title: Preah Khan, Angkor, Cambodia
highlights:
Preah Khan, Angkor, Cambodia

### Naga Bridge

West Causeway Preah Khan, Angkor The west causeway of Preah Khan is anchored by a naga bridge, which represents the Churning of the Sea of Milk; the east causeway is similarly bridged. In this photograph, a team of devas pulls Vasuki, whose menacing heads tower over the visitor. | Two boundary stones mark this corner of the bridge. |

title: Nāga
highlights: Naga people  ...    and Pali literature. They are generally represented as a class of superhumans taking the form of serpents who inhabit a subterranean world. Texts such as Manimekalai represent them as persons in human form. ### Cambodia Nāga statue front of Angkor Wat in Siem Reap province. Cambodian seven-headed nāga at the Royal Palace in Phnom Penh.  In Khmer culture, nāgas symbolize rain, and represent a bridge between the mortal realm (ឋានមនុស្ស) and the realm of devas(Heaven; ឋានទេវតា/ឋានសួគ៌). They have the  ...    intentions. Furthermore, Cambodian nāgas possess numerological symbolism based on the number of their heads. Odd-headed nāgas embody masculinity, infinity, timelessness, and immortality, since all odd numbers derives from  ...    - Depictions of Nagas in the area of Angkor
